does: This paper investigates backdoor vulnerabilities in Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) transformers by proposing BadSwitch, a novel attack framework that exploits the sparse routing mechanisms inherent to MoE architectures. The authors demonstrate that backdoors can be injected during pretraining by jointly optimizing trigger embeddings while identifying the most sensitive experts through a sensitivity-guided Top-S expert tracing mechanism. BadSwitch constrains the Top-K gating mechanism to route poisoned inputs through targeted expert pathways, embedding malicious triggers directly into the routing architecture rather than relying on superficial data poisoning or post-hoc model editing. The work provides empirical evidence that MoE models are vulnerable to such attacks and that task-coupled trigger optimization can achieve effective backdoor insertion with minimal detection.
connects: This work extends research on backdoor attacks in large language models by identifying a previously underexplored vulnerability class specific to MoE architectures. It builds on foundational backdoor attack literature while incorporating insights from recent work on model routing mechanisms and expert specialization in transformer networks. The paper connects to prior research on trigger optimization techniques and poisoning-based attacks, but distinguishes itself by targeting the sparse routing mechanism rather than standard dense layers. The approach also relates to work on understanding expert dynamics in MoE models and how architectural design choices create new attack surfaces. By focusing on task affinity between triggers and expert specializations, the research draws implicit connections to studies on how individual experts develop task-specific representations.
enables: This research opens several important directions for future work on defending MoE models against routing-based attacks. It motivates the development of detection mechanisms specifically designed for identifying anomalous expert routing patterns and trigger embeddings that exploit architectural vulnerabilities. The work enables research into robust expert gating mechanisms that resist adversarial manipulation of routing decisions while maintaining computational efficiency. It points toward understanding fundamental security properties of sparse routing architectures and whether similar vulnerabilities exist in other efficient model designs. Additionally, this attack framework could facilitate empirical evaluation of defense mechanisms and motivate careful consideration of backdoor risks during MoE model design and pretraining. The findings suggest that future work should examine the interaction between model architecture and security properties more explicitly.
